coredata.dc:title: Influence of nanoparticle shapes in nanofluid film boiling on vertical cylinders: A numerical study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yahyaee, A.
coredata.dc:description:
This research explores how nanoparticle shapes affect the thermal behavior of nanofluid film boiling along a vertical cylinder, utilizing a Continuous-Species-Transfer method embedded in a Computational Multi-Fluid Dynamics framework. By modeling the behavior of nanoparticles in both liquid and vapor phases, with considerations for Brownian motion and thermophoresis, a comprehensive 2D axisymmetric analysis is conducted. The analysis focuses on the effects of various Al
2
O
3
nanoparticle shapes – spheres, bricks, blades, cylinders, and platelets – on boiling heat transfer performance. Results demonstrate that while nanoparticle shape has a minimal impact on deposition dynamics, it significantly affects kinematic viscosity, particularly for platelet-shaped nanoparticles. Blade-shaped nanoparticles stand out for their substantial enhancement of thermal transport, as demonstrated by pronounced temperature gradients and elevated thermal conductivity. Furthermore, these nanoparticles contribute to a notable improvement in the Nusselt Number, suggesting enhanced efficiency in boiling heat transfer. This improvement is particularly significant at higher nanoparticle concentrations, where the effects are more pronounced, though not strictly linear when compared to the effects at lower concentrations. Future work will explore different nanoparticle materials, boiling scenarios, and experimental validation.

head.ce:title: Influence of nanoparticle shapes in nanofluid film boiling on vertical cylinders: A numerical study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This research explores how nanoparticle shapes affect the thermal behavior of nanofluid film boiling along a vertical cylinder, utilizing a Continuous-Species-Transfer method embedded in a Computational Multi-Fluid Dynamics framework. By modeling the behavior of nanoparticles in both liquid and vapor phases, with considerations for Brownian motion and thermophoresis, a comprehensive 2D axisymmetric analysis is conducted. The analysis focuses on the effects of various Al
2
O
3
nanoparticle shapes – spheres, bricks, blades, cylinders, and platelets – on boiling heat transfer performance. Results demonstrate that while nanoparticle shape has a minimal impact on deposition dynamics, it significantly affects kinematic viscosity, particularly for platelet-shaped nanoparticles. Blade-shaped nanoparticles stand out for their substantial enhancement of thermal transport, as demonstrated by pronounced temperature gradients and elevated thermal conductivity. Furthermore, these nanoparticles contribute to a notable improvement in the Nusselt Number, suggesting enhanced efficiency in boiling heat transfer. This improvement is particularly significant at higher nanoparticle concentrations, where the effects are more pronounced, though not strictly linear when compared to the effects at lower concentrations. Future work will explore different nanoparticle materials, boiling scenarios, and experimental validation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent academic focus on enhancing thermal efficiency in nanofluid boiling processes underscores its diverse applications in sectors ranging from large vehicle propulsion and power generation to cooling systems in advanced electronics and aerospace thermal management. Prior studies have largely concentrated on pool [1,2] and flow boiling [3,4] of nanofluids. Yet, the specific area of film boiling within nanofluids emerges as a critical, albeit less explored domain. Given the extreme thermal conditions in certain industrial applications, a thorough comprehension of film boiling is essential [5,6]. The incorporation of nanoparticles into fluids introduces additional complexity, warranting both computational [7] and experimental investigations [8,9].

Following Maxwell’s theoretical framework [10], adding particles to base fluids like water or oil can enhance thermal transfer properties [11,12]. However, microparticles face challenges such as sedimentation and clogging in conduits [13]. Nanofluids, formulated by combining base liquids with nanoparticles, were introduced to overcome these limitations [14]. Nanofluids exhibit altered thermal properties compared to their base fluids. These changes can include enhanced thermal conductivity, modified surface tension, and altered dynamic viscosity, offering potential advantages for various thermal applications. However, it is crucial to note that nanoparticle addition can also lead to a decrease in effective heat capacity. Therefore, careful consideration and evaluation are necessary to determine the suitability of nanofluids for specific thermal applications, taking into account both the desired property enhancements and potential trade-offs [12,15,16].

Nanoparticle characteristics substantially influence surface interactions and fluid dynamics in nanofluid boiling processes. One notable effect is on heated surfaces, where nanoparticle sedimentation occurs, affecting key factors like surface wettability, heat transfer coefficients, and capillary effects [17–19]. This sedimentation creates a layer that increases the effective contact area between phases, leading to improved heat transfer rates during phase change [20]. Moreover, nanoparticles are instrumental in defining wettability, which consequently alters boiling behaviors. Critical parameters such as the triple line radius and contact angle are determined by the balance of forces among the liquid, gas, and solid at the three-phase interface [21,22]. The composition and concentration of nanoparticles are key in modifying surface tension and contact angles, with silica nanofluids, for example, exhibiting lower surface tension than water, thereby influencing boiling characteristics like bubble departure size [23–25].

The impact of nanoparticles on droplet evaporation and bubble formation is also dependent on their size and type. Smaller nanoparticles tend to distribute more uniformly along the edge, while larger ones concentrate at the edges, affecting the evaporation dynamics [26]. Gold nanoparticles, in particular, have been observed to pin the triple line, significantly influencing bubble dynamics [27]. This phenomenon is further corroborated by research on various nanofluids, indicating that the contact line can extend substantially, sometimes by as much as 50 times the nanoparticles’ diameter. This expansion is attributed to increased structural disjoining pressures, affecting both spreading behavior and bubble formation [28–30].

Advancements in CFD research related to nanofluid boiling have significantly progressed, with initial studies focusing on heat transfer enhancements and recent investigations integrating nanoparticle movement equations. Early research in this domain includes Abedini et al.’s exploration of subcooled nanofluid flow boiling [31], Shoghl et al.’s analysis of bubble dynamics in nanofluid pool boiling [32], and Qi et al.’s study on the effects of TiO
2
nanoparticles in water-based nanofluids [13]. Abdollahi et al. examined the influence of various nanoparticle types and concentrations on laminar boiling in micro-channels [33], while Mohammed et al. confirmed the significant effect of nanoparticle concentration on heat transfer in diverse boiling scenarios [34,35].

Further developments include Soleimani et al.’s observations of improved thermal management with HFE-7100 nanofluids [36], and Li et al.’s investigation into the gravitational effects on boiling fluids [37]. Pournaderi et al. focused on Al
2
O
3
-Water nanofluid droplets, noting enhanced heat extraction at specific concentrations [38]. Zaboli et al. and Alomar et al. extended research to nanoparticle concentrations in silica and salt solution nanofluids [39,40], while Bahiraei et al. provided insights into entropy generation and exergy in Ag–Water nanofluid flow [41].

In the pursuit of advanced thermal management solutions, the interplay between thermophoresis and Brownian motion in nanofluids has garnered significant attention. Thermophoresis refers to the movement of particles from regions of higher temperature to lower temperature within a fluid, driven by thermal gradients. Brownian motion, on the other hand, pertains to the random movement of particles suspended in a fluid, attributed to collisions with fluid molecules. While several mechanisms can contribute to nanoparticle slip in nanofluids, Buongiorno [42] highlighted Brownian diffusion and thermophoresis as having a considerably larger impact compared to other terms. Building on this, Yang et al. [43] extended the Buongiorno model to include the migration effects of nanoparticles, shedding light on the anomalous heat transfer enhancements in nanofluids during forced convection. The combined effects of Brownian motion and thermophoresis not only enhance the thermal conductivity of nanofluids but also play a crucial role in optimizing the distribution and stability of nanoparticles, thereby significantly influencing the overall heat transfer efficiency.

Malvandi et al. [44] further explored the intricacies of mixed convection, revealing the nuanced impact of nanoparticle movement on temperature distributions and the overall thermal performance. This investigation brought to light the critical role of nanoparticles in shaping non-uniform distributions that significantly influence heat transfer in nanofluids. The work of Malvandi and Ganji [45] emphasized the dominance of Brownian motion in dictating nanoparticle migration within cooled channels, thereby affecting the transfer rates in Al
2
O
3
-Water nanofluids.

Complementing these findings, Michaelides [46] delved into thermophoresis effects, in various base liquids. Their research demonstrated how molecular impulses in hotter regions could drive particles towards cooler regions, thereby affecting the properties of nanofluids. Malvandi [47] examined the anisotropic behavior of thermal conductivity in magnetic nanofluids, highlighting the significant influence of Brownian motion and thermophoresis, especially in the presence of an external magnetic field.

Expanding on these themes, Malvandi et al. [48] offered a detailed account of how thermophoresis and Brownian motion contribute to the heat transfer enhancement in film boiling over vertical cylinders. They concluded that Al
2
O
3
nanoparticles, with their distinct migration patterns, could outperform titania nanoparticles in terms of cooling efficiency. This research was complemented by Makinde and Animasaun [49] who examined bioconvection in Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) nanofluids, revealing that Brownian motion and thermophoresis significantly affect reactant concentrations and the heat and mass transfer characteristics.

In the exploration of nanofluid dynamics, the work of Kumaran and Sandeep [50] delved into the effects of thermophoresis and Brownian motion on MHD Williamson and Casson fluids. Williamson fluids, characterized by their shear-thinning behavior, and Casson fluids, noted for their yield stress and plasticity, represent complex non-Newtonian fluids whose flow and heat transfer characteristics under magnetic fields are significantly influenced by nanoparticle motion. The study illustrated how these mechanisms alter the flow profiles and heat transfer rates, enhancing the thermal efficiency of these fluids in the presence of a magnetic field. Complementing this, Lin and Jiang [51] conducted a numerical simulation showcasing how Brownian motion and thermophoresis disrupt the equilibrium of nanoparticles in a fluid, leading to a marked improvement in heat transfer.

In the context of convective flows, Ghadikolaei et al. [52] incorporated Brownian motion and thermophoresis in their study of MHD Casson nanofluid over an inclined stretching sheet, finding significant impacts on flow profiles and overall heat transfer rates. Similarly, Ramana Reddy et al. [53] illustrated the critical role of these phenomena in unsteady MHD nanofluid flow over a stretching surface, with thermophoretic and Brownian motion parameters influencing fluid temperature and concentration profiles.

Mittal and Patel [54] delved into this complex interaction through an exploration of mixed convective MHD stagnation point flow in Casson fluids. Their study underscores the profound influence these mechanisms wield on temperature gradients and the consequent enhancement of heat and mass transfer processes. Building upon these foundational insights, Nadeem et al. [55] offered a comprehensive examination of viscoelastic fluid flow over a nonlinearly stretching sheet. Incorporating thermophoresis and Brownian motion into their boundary layer approximations, they were able to model the nuanced effects on velocity and thermal profiles, illustrating the substantial role these parameters play in determining fluid behavior.

Further emphasizing the critical nature of these mechanisms, Tawade et al. [56] investigated the laminar flow of Casson nanofluid across a stretching sheet, with a particular focus on thermal radiation and chemical reactions. Their analysis revealed that thermophoresis and Brownian motion not only alter the thermal boundary layer thickness but also significantly escalate temperature profiles, corroborating the findings of previous studies. In a practical application of these theoretical advancements, Wang et al. [57] tackled the challenge of nanoparticle deposition within microchannel cooling systems. Their work illuminated the dualistic effects of thermophoresis and Brownian motion, offering strategies to curtail deposition—a critical consideration in the thermal management of high-tech electronic apparatus. The aforementioned studies collectively underscore the significant impact of thermophoresis and Brownian motion on the heat transfer capabilities of nanofluids.

A growing body of research has been dedicated to exploring the impact of nanoparticle shapes on the thermophysical properties and heat transfer characteristics of nanofluids. Ghadikolaei et al. [58] investigate the thermophysical properties of TiO
2
-Cu-Water hybrid nanofluids, emphasizing the role of shape factors in MHD stagnation point flow. Their numerical analysis underlines the critical influence of nanoparticle shape on thermal conductivity enhancement, solving nonlinear differential equations numerically to capture the complex flow behavior. Similarly, Hamid et al. [59] analyze MoS
2
nanoparticles’ shape effects on rotating nanofluid flow along a stretching surface. Incorporating magnetic fields and variable thermal conductivity, they use the Galerkin method to tackle the model’s complexity, obtaining stable results for various hydrodynamic and heat transfer parameters.

In the domain of ferrofluids, Sheikholeslami and Shehzad [60] explore Fe
3
O
4
-Water nanofluid flow in permeable media under external magnetic sources. Their study reveals that platelet-shaped nanoparticles yield the highest heat transfer rate, employing the vorticity stream function formulation to assess shape effects on nanofluid properties. Liu et al. [61] explored the effects of nanoparticle shapes on Al
2
O
3
-Water nanofluids’ laminar forced convective heat transfer in curved square ducts. Introducing two new shape descriptors, flatness and elongation, the study found that platelet-shaped nanoparticles yielded the highest heat transfer enhancement, followed by cylinder, blade, sphere, and brick shapes. Non-spherical nanoparticles, especially those with a suitable shape, small size, and high volume fraction, were beneficial for heat transfer enhancement. Jiang et al. [62] investigated the influence of nanoparticle shapes on mixed forced and thermocapillary convection in minichannels. The study utilized a two-phase mixture model to examine the flow characteristics and heat transfer enhancement mechanism with various shaped nanoparticles (sphere, brick, blade, cylinder, and platelet). Findings indicated that nanoparticle shape significantly affects thermocapillary jet structure and thermal transport near bubbles, with blade-shaped nanoparticles enhancing the average Nusselt number by 8.3% compared to spherical ones at a nanoparticle volume fraction of 0.05.

Sahoo [63] focus on the thermo-hydraulic characteristics of radiators employing nanoparticle-based ternary hybrid nanofluids. They conclude that particle shape significantly impacts performance, with spherical, cylindrical, and platelet shapes enhancing heat transfer rates and second law efficiency in radiators. Rashid et al. [64] study (Ag and TiO
2
)-Water hybrid nanofluid flow towards a stretching shrinking horizontal cylinder, considering the shape effect of nanoparticles. Employing the Homotopy Analysis Method (HAM), they highlight the significant influence of suction and injection parameters on flow and heat transfer. Khashi’ie et al. [65] delve into radiative Cu-Al
2
O
3
-Water hybrid nanofluid flow towards an Electromagnetohydrodynamics (EMHD) plate, assessing different nanoparticles’ shape factors. Their findings indicate blade-shaped nanoparticles optimize heat transfer rates, leveraging similarity transformation and existing correlations to estimate thermophysical properties.

Saranya and Al-Mdallal [66] conduct a computational study on Al
2
O
3
-silicon oil nanofluid flow over a radially stretching rotating disk, examining nanoparticle shape effects. Their findings underscore the diverse impact of nanoparticle shapes on dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of nanofluid dynamics. Similarly, Shanmugapriya et al. [67] explored the heat transfer flow of magnetized ternary hybrid nanofluid over a stretching surface, emphasizing the remarkable heat transfer rate enhancement achieved through the use of blade-shaped nanoparticles. This study employed the Runge–Kutta–Fehlberg method, underlining the significant influence of nanoparticle geometry on thermal performance.

Adnan et al. [68] focused on the magneto-radiated annular fin performance using advanced ternary nanofluid, considering nanoparticle shape factors. Their findings demonstrated the efficacy of ternary nanomaterials in enhancing fin performance under natural convection, significantly influenced by nanoparticle shapes. In another study, El-sayed et al. [69] provided computational insights into the heat transport enhancement in MHD-free convection of polar ternary hybrid nanofluid around a radiant sphere. They found that blade-shaped nanoparticles exhibited the highest thermal conductivity ratio, affirming the pivotal role of nanoparticle shape in optimizing heat transfer processes.

These studies collectively underscore the profound impact of nanoparticle shapes on the heat transfer capabilities of nanofluids and also reveal a gap in understanding the specific influence of nanoparticle shapes on thermal performance during nanofluid boiling.
The review of existing literature reveals several pivotal gaps, which form the foundation for the current study:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Despite Buongiorno’s detailed analysis of forces such as Brownian diffusion and thermophoresis affecting nanoparticles [42], a considerable part of CFD research [13,31–40] still lacks the incorporation of dedicated nanoparticle transport equations. Moreover, even in CFD studies that do include these equations [43–46,48,51], there is a tendency to focus predominantly on the insulating role of the vapor layer in film boiling, often overlooking the critical interaction between the nanofluid’s liquid phase and the vapor layer.
Despite extensive CFD and experimental research, the effect of nanoparticle shapes on thermal performance during boiling is yet to be fully explored.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to these gaps, this study introduces a CFD analysis of film boiling in nanofluids, emphasizing the effect of various nanoparticle shapes like spheres, bricks, blades, cylinders, and platelets in water-based nanofluids. The CFD approach utilizes the Continuous-Species-Transfer (CST) methodology in Computational Multi-Fluid Dynamics (CMFD), providing an accurate simulation of crucial aspects of film boiling. Film boiling is the chosen study regime as it involves direct contact between the vapor layer and the heated solid surface, with the liquid phase remaining separated. This aspect minimizes the effects of changes in the triple line during nanofluid boiling. Brownian motion and thermophoresis are considered to enhance the CST methodology’s effectiveness in simulating nanofluid film boiling. The primary aim is to perform a detailed CFD analysis on film boiling of nanofluids, with a unique emphasis on the role of nanoparticle shapes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assumptions in the formulation of governing equations
ce:section.ce:para: The governing equations in the CFD model utilizing the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method are predicated on several assumptions:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The single-fluid or mixture model assumption is utilized, where the liquid and vapor phases move with a unified velocity field U and a single pressure field p , indicating equilibrium between phases.
Interfacial forces are represented by the Continuous Surface Force (CSF) model, assuming a well-defined and continuous interface.
Phase fractions are treated as smooth and continuous functions over computational cells.

The relative velocity is modeled as a compressive velocity
U
c
, artificially introduced to maintain sharp interfaces and reduce numerical diffusion.
Surface tension forces are computed assuming that the interface curvature κ and surface tension coefficient σ are accurately definable in each computational cell.
The compressive velocity model presumes the interfacial relative velocity to be normal to the interface, with its magnitude managed to avert excessive interfacial compression or rarefaction.

The thermophysical properties of the base fluid (both vapor and liquid phases) and the nanoparticles are assumed to remain constant, unaffected by the 4-degree temperature difference encountered in the study.

ce:section.ce:para: These assumptions are integral to the model’s simplification and are contingent on the physical system’s specifics and the simulation’s accuracy requirements.

The current research employs the VOF method to analyze the dynamics of a two-phase system, specifically focusing on the phase transitions involving thermal changes. This method bases its analysis on a unified set of conditionally volume-averaged conservation laws, applicable to both the liquid and vapor phases of the nanofluid. The core equations that govern the system dynamics are presented as follows:

The problem under investigation is depicted in Fig. 1, where a vertical cylinder is immersed in a nanofluid. The process commences when the cylinder’s surface temperature,
T
wall
, surpasses the boiling point,
T
saturat
. This leads to the formation of a vapor layer enveloping the cylinder, extending vertically along its length. The movement of this vapor layer along the cylinder’s z -axis is driven by buoyancy forces. In the model, the movement of the vapor is primarily influenced by the force of gravity.

Here, the complexity of the three-dimensional (3D) scenario is reduced to a two-dimensional (2D) axisymmetric representation for computational efficiency. The domain is configured as a wedge, where the z -axis, located at the cylinder’s vertical midpoint, acts as the axis of rotation. In this setup, the front and back boundaries of the domain are treated as ‘wedge’ types in OpenFOAM. This designation is crucial for simulating axisymmetric physical phenomena with enhanced computational efficiency.

The numerical model initiates with a thin vapor film in direct contact with the surface of the cylinder. The prescribed boundary conditions for this simulation are depicted in Fig. 1. The cylinder’s surface, representing the left boundary in the context of the simulation, is governed by a concentration boundary condition for the nanoparticles as encapsulated by a specific equation, seen in Fig. 1. The coefficients for thermophoretic (
D
Thps
) and Brownian diffusion (
D
Bn
) in that specific equation are quantified by Eqs. (A.2) and (A.3), respectively. The boundary condition applied at the cylinder wall is based on the principle that nanoparticles cannot penetrate the wall, ensuring that the net flux of nanoparticles at this boundary is zero. This zero-flux condition accounts for the collective migration of nanoparticles, known as Stefan flow, influenced by differences in both concentration and temperature. This concept is grounded in the investigations of Avramenko et al. as elaborated in their studies [48,80,81].

The boundary conditions for the computational model were carefully established to replicate the film boiling process along a vertical cylinder, isolating it from external environmental influences. Referencing foundational studies by Avramenko et al. [80,81] and Malvandi et al. [48], these conditions were designed to capture the internal thermal and fluid dynamics accurately, focusing on the essence of the boiling phenomenon:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The right boundary of the model is configured to represent an extensive medium, reducing the impact of the cylinder. Treated as an open boundary, it ensures minimal changes in temperature, volume fraction, and velocity, offering a simulation environment largely free from external influences. This setup is critical for a precise examination of the boiling phenomena, ensuring the analysis remains unaltered by external factors.

The top and bottom boundaries of the model are set as open. This configuration eliminates artificial barriers to boiling and vapor movement, providing a clearer and unaltered representation of film boiling dynamics.

ce:section.ce:para: Through the application of these boundary conditions, the study seeks to thoroughly examine the mechanisms of film boiling within a controlled environment, reducing external impacts to highlight the fundamental behavior of nanofluid boiling.

Film boiling of nanofluids near supercritical conditions is a phenomenon that finds its application in several high-end industrial processes. The typical geometry involving a vertical cylinder is a reflection of practical systems where such conditions are prevalent.

In the field of aerospace, for instance, the cooling of propulsion system components, which may operate under near supercritical conditions, is essential. The cylindrical geometries are common in these components, and the advanced thermal management provided by nanofluids is crucial for maintaining operational integrity and safety.

Another application is found in the cooling of superconducting magnets, which are critical components in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) systems and particle accelerators. These magnets require cooling systems that can function effectively at near supercritical states to maintain superconductivity. Nanofluids, with their superior thermal properties, offer a promising solution for such high-stake applications.

The chemical processing industry also benefits from the superior heat transfer properties of nanofluids. During the manufacturing of chemicals and materials, processes often approach supercritical states requiring robust cooling strategies. Here, the application of film boiling of nanofluids around cylindrical geometries can significantly enhance the efficiency of heat exchangers.

Moreover, in high-energy physics and engineering applications such as particle accelerators and plasma research, where equipment often operates at extreme temperatures and pressures, the cooling systems must be capable of handling near supercritical conditions. Nanofluids offer a potent solution due to their elevated thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficients, providing reliable cooling for cylindrical equipment used in these sectors.

In all these contexts, nanofluids not only enhance thermal efficiency but also contribute to the compact design and improved operational stability of heat exchange systems. Their application becomes particularly relevant under extreme conditions, where traditional cooling methods might falter, and film boiling emerges as a critical heat transfer mode. Understanding nanofluid film boiling in such scenarios is essential for optimizing system design and ensuring reliability in high-stakes environments. This investigation into nanofluid film boiling, especially in the vicinity of supercritical conditions and around vertical cylinders, underscores the importance of preparedness for extreme operational scenarios, highlighting the need for detailed studies in this domain.

In the current analysis, a diverse array of nanoparticle geometries are used, including spheres, blades, bricks, cylinders, and platelets, as delineated in Fig. 2. Each nanoparticle shape is standardized to have an equivalent spherical diameter (
d
np
) of 45 nm for consistency. The term ’equivalent spherical diameter’ refers to the diameter of a sphere that embodies the same volume as the irregularly shaped particle. The detailed geometrical specifications for each nanoparticle form are based on the seminal definitions provided by the source [62].

For the numerical simulations to yield reliable results, it is crucial to employ accurate models for the nanofluid’s thermophysical properties. Contrary to the simpler rule of mixture, which has been deemed inadequate by the findings of Das et al. [82], a more sophisticated approach is required to compute these properties for nanofluids. In the present investigation, the computational formulae for calculating the nanofluid’s density and specific heat capacity are as follows:

The chosen correlations for determining the thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity of nanofluids are grounded in widely recognized models that link these thermophysical properties to the shape of nanoparticles. The adoption of these correlations is validated by their application in various studies [61–63,69], where they have been instrumental in predicting the adjustments in thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity due to the presence and specific shapes of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mesh and time step independence studies
ce:section.ce:para:
A comprehensive mesh independence study was initiated to determine the optimal grid size for the computational analyses. The study aimed to capture the vapor film interface with high fidelity at a specific time instance across a spectrum of mesh densities, designated as
N
grids
=
[ 50 × 50 , 100 × 100 , 200 × 200 , 300 × 300 , 400 × 400 ]
. The grid generation strategy employed a simpleGrading scheme with a ratio of
( 15 , 1 , 1 )
in OpenFOAM to ensure a refined mesh near the heated wall, which is critical for accurately resolving the vapor film interface. Fig. 5 presents the comparative accuracy of the vapor film interface for these varying grid resolutions.

After careful consideration of computational efficiency and accuracy, a grid resolution of
300 × 300
was chosen for subsequent simulations. This grid size closely mirrors the precision offered by the
400 × 400
grid while significantly reducing computational resources.

Following the mesh independence analysis, a Courant number-based time step independence study was conducted. The temporal resolution in computational fluid dynamics simulations is critically governed by the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition. This condition is a stability criterion for numerical schemes, encapsulated by the formula:
(21)
Co =
U
max
Δ t
Δ x
,
where Co is the Courant number,
U
max
represents the maximum flow velocity,
Δ t
is the time step, and
Δ x
is the smallest spatial grid dimension. The CFL condition dictates that for numerical stability, the Courant number should ideally not exceed 1, meaning physical information should not travel more than one grid cell per time step. This ensures that the numerical domain of dependence encompasses the physical domain of dependence, preventing information from propagating too quickly through the computational grid and leading to numerical instabilities.

In this study, a grid size of
300 × 300
with simpleGrading (15 1 1) in OpenFOAM was selected to ensure a fine resolution near the wall where gradients are steepest. By examining Fig. 6, the Courant number of 0.005, although less than the CFL limit of 1, was chosen after comparative analysis with smaller Courant numbers. This specific value is a balance between maintaining numerical stability (as per the CFL condition) and computational efficiency. The conservative choice of 0.005 minimizes any possible discretization errors while providing an accurate representation of the fluid’s temporal evolution. Fig. 6 confirms that simulations with
Co = 0 . 005
provide results that are in close agreement with those obtained at
Co = 0 . 001
, indicating that the precision of temporal discretization is sufficiently captured without unnecessary computational cost.

This study employs dimensionless numbers to standardize the physical quantities, enabling a universal comparison of thermal phenomena across various conditions and scales. This normalization is crucial for transcending the limitations imposed by specific dimensional units, thereby facilitating the broader applicability of the findings.

This study examines the effect of Al
2
O
3
nanoparticle shapes – sphere, brick, blade, cylinder, and platelet – on the thermal performance of film boiling along a vertical cylinder, utilizing water-based nanofluids. The primary focus is to assess how these distinct nanoparticle shapes impact the boiling process, specifically in terms of nanoparticle deposition patterns, temperature profiles, and variations in key thermophysical properties. Also, one of the pivotal aspects of this analysis is the evaluation of the Nusselt number across different nanoparticle shapes. This metric serves as a crucial indicator of the efficiency of heat transfer enhancements attributable to the nanoparticles.

Fig. 7 reveals the nuanced behavior of nanoparticle deposition profiles adjacent to the heated wall during the boiling of nanofluids. The investigation sets the nanoparticle concentration in the nanofluid at
1 × 1
0
− 2
, aiming to uncover the influence of nanoparticle shape on deposition dynamics. Despite varying shapes – spherical, brick, blade, cylinder, and platelet – the observed deposition patterns do not exhibit significant differences. This uniformity across multiple non-dimensional time steps,
t
∗
, suggests that the geometric configuration of the nanoparticles does not play a significant role in dictating deposition behavior within the boiling regime.

Another observation emerges from the time-dependent analysis of deposition rates. Independent of the nanoparticle shape, there is a noticeable increase in deposition over time. This phenomenon underscores an aspect of nanofluid boiling processes which is the temporal aspect of nanoparticle deposition on the surface.

The gradient near the wall, initially low, suggesting a near-uniform nanoparticle concentration layer, becomes progressively steeper further from the wall. This changing gradient underscores the complicated dynamics of nanoparticle migration, surface interactions, and the thermal gradients arising from the boiling process. Moreover, the deposition patterns observed here are similar to those reported by Malvandi et al. [48], lending further credence to the findings presented.

The vicinity of the heated wall is a critical area in the boiling behavior of nanofluids, significantly affecting the thermal transfer efficiency. This is also supported by the calculation of the Nusselt number (Eq. (27)). Figs. 8, 9, 10, and 11 present the extracted density, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and kinematic viscosity of the vapor nanofluid, influenced by the nanoparticle shapes. These properties are probed at a normalized axial position of
x
∗
= 40
, illustrating their variation in the immediate vicinity of the heated wall.

The observations from Figs. 8 and 9 indicate that while the density and specific heat capacity of the nanofluid are influenced by the presence of nanoparticles, these properties do not significantly vary across different nanoparticle shapes. This outcome is in line with predictions from Eqs. (13) and (14), highlighting that nanoparticle concentration plays a pivotal role in defining these thermophysical properties, rather than the shape of the nanoparticles. Earlier observations from Fig. 7 also corroborate this, showing that the deposition and concentration profiles of nanoparticles – regardless of whether they are spherical, brick, blade, cylinder, or platelet – do not significantly differ. Consequently, the consistent behavior of density and specific heat capacity across different nanoparticle shapes, as shown in Figs. 8 and 9, is substantiated by these combined insights.

Physically, the density of a nanofluid affects its buoyancy and flow behavior, while the specific heat capacity determines its ability to store and transfer heat. The observed insensitivity of these properties to nanoparticle shape suggests that the density and specific heat capacity behavior of nanofluids in boiling processes is more strongly governed by nanoparticle concentration than by the geometric nuances of individual particles.

As shown in Figs. 10 and 11, the thermal conductivity and viscosity characteristics of nanofluids are profoundly influenced by the shape of nanoparticles. Blade-shaped nanoparticles enhance thermal conductivity by 1.38%, facilitating improved heat transfer capabilities of the nanofluid. A higher thermal conductivity implies that the fluid can conduct heat away from the hot surface more effectively, which is beneficial in cooling applications. The corresponding 3.73% increase in kinematic viscosity indicates a higher resistance to flow, which can lead to an intensified boundary layer effect and potentially augmented convective heat transfer rates due to the increased interaction between the fluid and the surface.

Similarly, brick-shaped nanoparticles, with a modest increase of 0.19% in thermal conductivity and 0.25% in kinematic viscosity, suggest slight improvements in heat transfer with minimal changes in flow characteristics. Cylindrical nanoparticles, with a 0.49% enhancement in thermal conductivity and a 4.10% rise in kinematic viscosity, present a balanced improvement in both thermal transfer and dynamic fluid behavior. On the other hand, platelet-shaped nanoparticles exhibit a 0.73% boost in thermal conductivity paired with a substantial 10.85% increase in kinematic viscosity. This significant rise in viscosity could lead to a more stable and coherent fluid layer adjacent to the heated surface, which, despite the potential for reduced fluidity, may enhance heat transfer in certain boiling regimes due to thicker thermal boundary layers.

While the increases in thermal conductivity across all shapes are relatively moderate, the pronounced differences in kinematic viscosity, particularly with platelet-shaped nanoparticles, have the potential to markedly affect the fluid’s flow dynamics. The resultant changes in the flow behavior could have implications for the boiling heat transfer mechanism, indicating that nanoparticle shape is a pivotal factor in tailoring nanofluids for specific thermal management applications.

It is worthy of mention that as expected the thermal conductivity outcomes corroborate with the Hamilton-Crosser model, where the empirical shape factor, denoted as n , exhibits an inverse relationship with the sphericity, Ψ . Sphericity quantifies the resemblance of a particle’s shape to a perfect sphere. A lower sphericity signifies a particle’s shape notably deviating from spherical geometry. This characteristic influences the nanofluid’s thermal conductivity, as illustrated in Eq. (15) and further detailed for each particle shape in Table 1. Concurrently, the viscosity findings align with the model captured in Eq. (17) and Table 2, taking into account the alterations in fluid dynamics induced by the diverse shapes of nanoparticles.

Fig. 12 illustrates the normalized temperature profiles for different nanoparticle shapes at a constant axial position (
x
∗
= 40
) on the cylinder’s vertical axis during nanofluid boiling. The profiles showcase a nuanced thermal behavior, characterized by a nearly linear decrease in temperature from the heated wall towards the bulk of the liquid. This linear trend is most pronounced near the wall, where thermal conduction is most effective, reflecting a higher temperature gradient due to the direct heat transfer from the heated surface. By moving away from the wall, a subtle curvature emerges within the profile, indicative of the diminishing rate of temperature decrease. This profile, resembling temperature profile characteristics for nanofluid boiling as seen in the works of Malvandi et al. [48] and Avramenko et al. [7], transitions towards a flatter gradient as it approaches the vapor–liquid interface, signifying a near-zero thermal gradient in this region. This pattern indicates that while conductive heat transfer dominates near the wall, the influence diminishes with distance, culminating in a balanced state at the interface where the liquid and vapor phases meet.

Among the nanoparticle shapes, blade-shaped nanoparticles exhibit the most pronounced temperature gradient, suggesting a more efficient thermal transport away from the wall. This observation is closely followed by platelet and cylinder shapes, which also display significant temperature gradients. Conversely, sphere and brick shapes reveal the least steep gradients, indicating their lower thermal transport capabilities. The observed variance in temperature gradients underscores the critical role of nanoparticle shape in influencing thermal transport phenomena. Blade and platelet shapes, associated with steeper temperature gradients, promote a more effective heat transfer away from the heated surface. In contrast, the milder slopes are observed for sphere and brick shapes.

The influence of nanoparticle shape and concentration ( φ ) on the space-averaged normalized Nusselt number (
Nu
¯
spav
) is analyzed, as detailed in Figs. 13, 14, and 15. At the lower nanoparticle concentration of
φ = 0 . 01
, the
Nu
¯
spav
values across different shapes are quite similar, indicating that shape has a less obvious impact on the enhancement of heat transfer.

With increasing nanoparticle concentration to
φ = 0 . 05
, the
Nu
¯
spav
profiles start to show a noticeable separation, pointing towards a growing influence of nanoparticle shape on the boiling heat transfer dynamics. This separation is even more distinct at a higher concentration of
φ = 0 . 1
, confirming a substantial correlation between nanoparticle shape and thermal performance during boiling.

When comparing the space-averaged normalized Nusselt number for different shapes of nanoparticles at a concentration of
φ = 0 . 1
against a pure fluid baseline (
φ = 0
), the findings reveal a significant variance in heat transfer enhancement across the nanoparticle shapes. Blade-shaped nanoparticles exhibit the highest increase in
Nu
¯
spav
by approximately 151.77%, followed by platelet-shaped nanoparticles with a 133.95% increase. Cylinder-shaped nanoparticles also show a considerable improvement in heat transfer efficiency with a 115.79% increase, while brick-shaped nanoparticles contribute to an 84.49% enhancement. Sphere-shaped nanoparticles, despite showing the least improvement, still enhance the
Nu
¯
spav
by 73.06%, highlighting the overall positive impact of nanoparticle shape on boiling heat transfer efficiency.

This observed trend corroborates with the thermal conductivity and temperature distribution data previously discussed (see Figs. 10 and 12). Over time, all shapes converge to a steady-state
Nu
¯
spav
.

These findings suggest that the thermal performance of nanofluids can be significantly tailored by selecting appropriate nanoparticle shapes, with blade and platelet shapes offering substantial benefits in applications requiring high-efficiency boiling heat transfer. For example, in cooling systems for high-power electronic components, leveraging blade-shaped nanoparticles could dramatically enhance heat dissipation, potentially improving device performance and lifespan. Similarly, in industrial processes where precise thermal management is critical, the strategic selection of nanoparticle shape based on these findings could lead to more efficient cooling solutions, optimizing energy consumption and process stability.

The investigation into the impact of nanoparticle shape – spherical, brick, blade, cylinder, or platelet – on the film boiling of nanofluids has elucidated several key aspects of thermal transfer and fluid dynamics. Specifically focusing on Al
2
O
3
nanoparticles of varying shapes dispersed in water, this study examined the consequent effects on the boiling process along a vertical cylinder. Advanced computational simulations, facilitated by the Continuous-Species-Transfer method within a Computational Multi-Fluid Dynamics framework, have significantly deepened the comprehension of the impact of nanoparticle shapes on the thermophysical attributes and boiling heat transfer efficiency of nanofluids.
From this comprehensive study, several important conclusions have been drawn:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Despite the variation in shapes, the impact of nanoparticle shape on deposition patterns near the heated wall during nanofluid boiling was found to be minimal. However, an increasing trend in nanoparticle deposition over time was observed, indicating that prolonged exposure leads to enhanced surface accumulation.

The density and specific heat capacity of nanofluids were observed to be invariant to changes in nanoparticle shape. This indicates that the buoyancy and thermal storage capacity of the nanofluid are primarily determined by nanoparticle concentration, rather than shape.

The shape of the nanoparticles significantly impacts the thermal conductivity and kinematic viscosity of nanofluids. Blade-shaped nanoparticles demonstrate the greatest improvement in thermal conductivity, while platelet-shaped nanoparticles show the most substantial increase in viscosity.

Temperature profile analysis across different nanoparticle shapes revealed that blade and platelet shapes are more efficient in thermal transport compared to other shapes, highlighting the critical role of nanoparticle shape in thermal boundary layer development.

The analysis of space-averaged normalized Nusselt numbers across varying nanoparticle shapes and concentrations revealed a significant role of nanoparticle shape in enhancing heat transfer, with blade-shaped nanoparticles showing the highest increase in heat transfer efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Drawing from these items, it is concluded that the shape of nanoparticles plays a pivotal role in the thermal dynamics of nanofluid film boiling. The nuanced understanding gained from this research can serve as a foundational basis for optimizing nanofluid applications in heat transfer processes, especially in scenarios requiring meticulous thermal management.

Future research directions can explore the dynamic behaviors of nanofluids in boiling processes across a variety of nanoparticle materials, examining their heat transfer enhancements. Also, studies can include diverse boiling scenarios, such as boiling from horizontal plates and flow boiling in different geometries. The experimental validation of computational findings stands as a crucial future step, potentially offering a robust link between theoretical insights and practical outcomes.

By combining Eqs. (A.5) and (A.6), a more robust model emerges. In situations where the velocity due to phase change is negligible compared to the velocity induced by gravitational forces, specific surface integral terms in the governing equations may be omitted. This assumption, which is supported by the literature [48,91], simplifies the model without compromising its integrity for the specific case of film boiling influenced predominantly by gravity. Thus, the model remains faithful to the physics of the problem while achieving computational efficiency.

Eq. (A.22) embodies a comprehensive model that is applicable across the entire computational domain. It adeptly represents the behavior of nanoparticles in both the nanofluid and vapor phases, as well as their interaction at the interface. This governing equation adeptly captures the standard nanoparticle transport phenomena, along with the specialized interface conditions, and addresses the contrasting properties of the two phases in the context of film boiling.
